region_of_origin: Singapore (Indian diaspora context; bread roll style originating from South Asia)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000F (Bread and rolls) used as closest defensible category for a soft wheat-flour bread roll; no specific code for Indian-style pav or dinner roll exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

allergens: May contain traces of dairy (butter or milk used in some variants)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of extra bread is 1 to 2 soft bread rolls weighing approximately 100 g, commonly served as a side accompaniment to dishes such as Pav Bhaji or Vada Pav in an Indian restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bread, white, commercially prepared (SR Legacy ID 18069)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for white commercially prepared bread (ID 18069), which is the closest approved-source equivalent to a soft wheat-flour Indian bread roll (pav/dinner roll). Input values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA data and replaced with USDA-sourced values as the approved-source anchor. Energy 265 kcal/100g (USDA) vs 280 kcal/100g (input); protein 8.9g vs 8.5g; fat 3.6g vs 4.5g; carbohydrate 49.2g vs 52.0g; sodium 491mg vs 450mg. USDA SR Legacy values adopted throughout. No exact match for Indian pav exists in HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN databases at the required level of detail.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 assigned based on white wheat bread entries in the peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). This peer-reviewed journal publication is an approved GI source. Soft Indian bread rolls (pav) are made from refined wheat flour (maida) with a composition and processing profile closely analogous to commercially prepared white bread. No direct GI measurement for Indian pav exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; white bread GI 75 from this peer-reviewed source is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (GI x available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (75 x 49.2) / 100 = 36.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 491 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; low potassium (115 mg/100 g) and modest fibre (2.3 g/100 g) provide insufficient offset to the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit extra bread to a single small roll per meal, choose lower-sodium wholemeal variants where available, and avoid pairing with high-sodium condiments such as butter or salted chutneys to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2,000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 75) and high glycemic load per serving (GL 36.9) from refined wheat flour (maida) with low dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g), driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit refined white bread rolls; if consumed, restrict to half a roll paired with a protein source or healthy fat to blunt the glycaemic response, and prefer wholemeal or high-fibre bread alternatives with GI below 55.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (491 mg/100 g) raises blood pressure risk; refined carbohydrate with low fibre contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation; saturated fat is low (0.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g, which are mitigating factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of refined white bread rolls due to high sodium and high glycaemic load; substituting with wholegrain or oat-based bread provides more fibre, lower GI, and better cardiovascular outcomes per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high glycaemic load promotes systemic inflammation; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants) present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute refined white bread rolls with wholegrain or sourdough alternatives to reduce pro-inflammatory glycaemic load and increase fibre and micronutrient density.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Extra Bread Roll is a soft, leavened wheat-flour bread roll served as an additional side accompaniment in Indian restaurant settings in Singapore, commonly paired with dishes such as Pav Bhaji (spiced mashed vegetable curry) or Vada Pav (fried potato fritter in a bun). The roll is made from refined wheat flour (maida), water, yeast, salt, sugar, and a small amount of oil or butter, then baked until golden and soft. The texture is pillowy and slightly chewy with a thin crust, and the flavour is mildly sweet and yeasty. Nutritionally, the bread roll is a high-carbohydrate, moderate-protein, low-fat food providing approximately 265 kcal per 100 g. It has a high glycemic index (GI approximately 75) due to its refined flour base and low dietary fibre content (2.3 g/100 g), making it a high glycaemic load food (GL approximately 36.9 per 100 g serving). Sodium is elevated at 491 mg/100 g from added salt in the dough. The roll is enriched with B-vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, folate) and iron through standard flour fortification. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without strict portion control. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-certified in most Indian restaurant contexts in Singapore. The product is analogous to a commercially prepared white dinner roll or pav.
common_user_queries: What is extra bread in Indian restaurants?
Is pav bread suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of Indian bread roll

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are anchored to USDA SR Legacy ID 18069 (white commercially prepared bread), which is the closest approved-source equivalent to a soft Indian wheat-flour bread roll. The input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and replaced with USDA-sourced values. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008; white bread GI 75). No exact match for Indian pav exists in HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN databases at the level of detail required; medium confidence reflects the adapted source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ID 18069 (white commercially prepared bread) as the closest approved-source equivalent; original GrabFood input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were not used as primary evidence per source policy. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy ID 18069; this bread roll contains no animal-derived fat in its standard formulation. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 mg/100g — naturally zero as refined wheat bread contains no ascorbic acid. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 mcg RAE/100g — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy ID 18069. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 mcg/100g — naturally zero as this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 mcg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy ID 18069. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat bread entries in the peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for Indian pav exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Sodium value of 491 mg/100g is from USDA SR Legacy; the input value of 450 mg/100g is within the plausible range but USDA value is adopted as the approved-source anchor. 5. Dietary flags: vegan and halal status reflect the standard formulation without butter or dairy; some restaurant variants may use butter, which would affect vegan and dairy-free status. 6. FoodEx2 code A000F used as closest defensible category; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for Indian-style pav or dinner roll. 7. All aliases_by_language string values confirmed present verbatim in the flat aliases array (self-check passed): english (9 entries), chinese (2), malay (1), tamil (2), hindi (3), romanized (1) — total 17 unique alias strings all present in flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Resolved g2 failure — confirmed nutrient_source organization and URL point exclusively to USDA FoodData Central (approved source); confirmed gi_source organization explicitly names Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal (approved GI source); removed all references to forbidden sources from source fields; cleaned up duplicate Tamil alias entries in flat aliases array from previous iteration.
